title: Share of adults who are obese vs. daily protein supply
subtitle: Obesity is defined as having a body-mass index (BMI) equal to, or greater than, 30. BMI is a person's weight (in kilograms) divided by their height (in meters) squared.
caption:
Share of adults who are obese vs. daily protein supply, 2022

Obesity is defined as having a body-mass index (BMI) equal to, or greater than, 30. BMI is a person's weight (in kilograms) divided by their height (in meters) squared.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between daily protein supply and adult obesity rates for countries worldwide in 2022. The horizontal axis measures daily protein supply in grams per day per capita, ranging from roughly 30 g to 150 g, while the vertical axis tracks the percentage of adults with obesity, spanning from 0% to over 70%. Each dot represents a country, color-coded by continent, revealing a visible positive correlation where nations with higher protein availability generally show higher obesity prevalence. At the upper extreme of the distribution, Pacific Island nations Nauru and Tonga occupy the highest positions on the vertical axis, with obesity rates exceeding 70% and protein supplies between 110 g and 130 g. Samoa and Kiribati also appear in this upper band, with obesity rates between 60% and 70%. In the middle of the range, the United States and Chile sit near the 120 g mark for protein, with obesity rates hovering around 35% to 45%. Conversely, the lower left quadrant contains numerous African nations such as Burundi, Somalia, and Rwanda, which cluster below 50 g of daily protein supply and maintain obesity rates under 10%. Asian countries like Vietnam, Nepal, and Bangladesh also appear in this lower band, with protein supplies around 70 g to 80 g and obesity rates below 10%. European nations including Iceland, Ireland, and Norway appear on the far right with high protein supplies exceeding 130 g, yet their obesity rates remain moderate, generally falling between 20% and 30%. The data points show that while low protein supply consistently coincides with low obesity rates, high protein supply corresponds with a wide variance in obesity outcomes.
